sentences.text: Right.
Well, it wasn't quite that we missed the car.
The car never came.
[LAUGHS]
The car didn't come, of course.
Well--
But the gentlemen looking-- the family looking after us couldn't keep us any longer.
So--
That was a Jewish family.
Jewish family.
So he explained that there's a train that goes to a certain place.
I cannot remember the place.
When you get there, you have to change trains and go onto the one that goes to Zagreb.
And they gave us ticket H.
Of course, the difficulty was it's not just like taking a journey from here to Melbourne or-- or anything like that.
The difficulty was that, first of all, we didn't speak the language.
We didn't read the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lettering.
They used the Russian alphabet or a similar one, so it was difficult to know--
Yugoslav.
Yeah.
So we sort of wrote the word in our hands [INAUDIBLE] to know where we had to change trains.
And he said, you'll find that most of the people are changing trains at that point anyhow.
The danger was that-- and he made us aware of it-- that we will be asked for identification.
And seeing that we had no papers, we had to concoct stories as to what we were doing there.
No.
So we came to the station.
The train arrived.
And so we decided-- we didn't have a whole sitting about it, but I mean, we all sat separately.
I mean, this comes back to the thing, each one for himself.
But we did keep contact in as much as we sat in the same car.
And we sat interspaced on different sides, but within view of each other.
And [SIGHS] so the trains went on.
Five, yes.
Five boys.
Of course, the inspector came to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ask for identification.
And I must say, they weren't that concerned with boys.
But nevertheless, they asked, what are you doing here?
And the stories were-- you know, my story was that I was here with my father who was a foreign worker.
A lot of German workers were working there.
And he just accepted that and walked away.
Oh, yes.
There was a lot of German spoken, because it was Croatia.
Croatia was-- there were a lot of Germans living in Croatia.
It was a fairly well-known-- you know, I mean, when he spoke to me, I said I speak German, you know.
And he-- he spoke German.
It so happened, that wasn't the spoken language.
But that didn't present a problem.
So when we came, we changed stations.
We did the same thing.
We went into another carriage and we sat within sight of each other.
And so it went on.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came to a station which was just outside Zagreb when we noticed-- we had noticed a few other people that were looked-- looked very much like refugees to us.
You know, we were children.
But a lot of adults were taking the same route to get out.
And--
No, no.
This is just a question of hours.
And the Croats delighted in catching people.
I suppose they were-- you know, the individuals were instructed to do that and to send them back to Germany.
Anyway, here we are at the station just before we're supposed to come to Zagreb.

And this gentleman-- just to give you an incident-- was pulled out by the scruff of his neck and put into this-- it so happened that our wagon was parked right outside the-- call it police station, whatever it was.
And they took a couple little parcels that he had with him away.
They took his papers away from him and all this.
And what we did notice was that as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the train was there and he was being interrogated, he kept on looking backwards.
[LAUGHS] And Vitus sort of winked at me.
And he said, watch this fellow.
And true enough, when the train pulled out, he just turned around, and he bolted out of the station and got onto the last carriage and came through.
Oh, yes.
I mean--
[LAUGHS] Go to [INAUDIBLE] junction, see if you can tell.
No, no.
We knew that--
No, but I mean, you could see what they were doing to him, that he was trying to-- you know, he was one of the people trying to escape.
And as I said before, there were many of them like that.
Well, I don't know.
Just knew.
I can't tell you what.
It wasn't written on him, you know.
So anyway, the problem was that the station was just one stop away from-- from Zagreb.
And we knew that it was really close, because by that time there wasn't just one rail-- what do you call it?
One rail line.
You could see the converging of numerous rail lines.
So we knew we weren't far away.
And he had to get out.
So what we did is we gave him one of our tickets, you know, the tickets, because that was a problem.
His problem was to get out past the person that collects the tickets.
And we tore one of them-- one of ours in half.
And we said, we'll squeeze in with all the people.
And that's what we did.
We got out.
Was he German?
Was a German Jewish man?
I've got no idea who it was.
Didn't speak to him.
Just--
Handed him a ticket.
--hand him a ticket and-- and that was it, you know.
Before we knew was-- he was out, and we were out.
And we went through the-- what do call it where the tickets are being collected?
The gates.
And we shoved half a ticket into the controller's hands and just got out.
So on getting out, we looked around.
And we soon were picked up by a couple of elderly-- elderly, they were in their 30's.
[LAUGHS] It's a bit of a joke when I say that now-- guys who knew to expect us.
And they took charge of us.
And we were then taken to-- again, there was an organization there.
And we found out later on that by that time, there was a collection of-- Recha Freier was actually sitting at that time in Zagreb.
There was a collection of close to 100 children that she was looking after in Zagreb.
And that was-- by that time, this was March, 1941.
In fact, I have a photo of that.
It's only a little one.
But I have a photo of that day.
Oh, yes.
We were area of it.
But I mean, that really was of no consequence at that time.
What happened there was of course we were aware.
Because then being in Zagreb, we had to find a means of keeping ourselves alive.
Now, the Jewish community there was rather small.
But they tried their best to help out with food and all this.
But we couldn't stay with them, because that was dangerous for them as well.
And so we were staying in halls.
And, you know, we're living underground and moved around different places.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to go on Shabbat for services.
We used to-- they used to pay us to be-- to come to a minyan or anything like that.
And we had also some other means.
I mean, we were-- we became very skilled at a little story that-- how do you get food?
Now, if you can't-- if you've got no money to buy it, and if you can't get any other way, you've got to invent a way of getting it.
And there are no orchards to pick apples from, but there was a market there.

And so we invented this game where we would run around the market and play chasings and accidentally get our legs caught into the-- where the farmers used to hand out the-- market their things, the tables they had there with trellises and all that.
And we would knock the trellises over and the food would go everywhere.
And, of course, we would help them pick it up.
But not everything would finish back up on the table.
And we would have a good feed.
Well, we--
No, no, no.
No, there was a whole group of kids.
I mean, like I said, there were 100 kids by then.
And they all had to make-- make their way.
I mean, the whole thing there, I was one of the last ones, I must tell you, of these kids to arrive.
The others were already there.
They had gone on similar trips, similar journeys previously.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was March, '41.
And we were there for a while.
I'm not quite sure whether it's three weeks, four weeks, whatever.
And at that point, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were told that Hitler's marched into or is marching into Yugoslavia and that we had to get out.
So [INAUDIBLE] picked-- these are our youth leaders-- picked us up off the street.
And I don't know how we're going for tape, but I mean--
Yeah.
Yeah.
And so quick, you have to get to the railway station, get the train.
We have to move on.
The whole group has to move on.
And I was on my way.
And I said to Vitus, I can't go.
Why can't you go?
Because I had a watch that I had given to-- it was stupidity, but it was still a very big sentimental value.
It was a gift from my mother.
I had given it to repair.
I said, I've got to get that first before I go.
And I went the other way, the opposite way, whatever time it took.
He came with me.
We went and got that watch.
And when we came back, we found that the train had actually left.
Luckily for us, there was another train left, which is a goods train.
And it was just about to go, so we jumped onto the back of that.
And we went out with it.
Well, who knows?
It was going out, you know.
And so but as it happened, further down the way, it caught up with the other one.
And we were reunited with-- with that train.
And we went from there to Belgrade.
We arrived there in April.
Belgrade was, of course, the opposite situation.
Belgrade was Serb.
And it's something that's reflected in today's politics.
Hated the Germans.
Couldn't even-- we weren't allowed to speak the German language, because we might have been taken as German nationals.
So that was difficult.
We were there, living under fairly difficult circumstances for a few days.
And the transport then went on to cross the border into Greece.
On another train.
We went to Greece.
It was a very short train.

And we were told-- or not us personally, but the-- the people leaving, you know, we heard through them that the Germans were coming in to Greece from the south and that we had to get out quickly, to get out the place.
And in order to facilitate the train going faster, they disconnected the last wagon, which contained our worldly possessions.
Now, we had been given in Zagreb a small suitcase with clothing.
And we were putting our things in there.
And so we lost all that again.
And the train sped off across the border into Turkey, across the Bosphorus, came to Istanbul.
About 100.
About 100.
And just with the possessions you had on you.
That's it.
And Istanbul, again, we stayed for two or three days.
I mean, there was no danger there anymore, just--
She was with us, yeah.
And then we continued by train right through Turkey, Syria, where we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Syria, Lebanon.
I think we were in Aleppo.
And later in April, it was Pesach '41.
And we arrived in Israel, the-- the Palestinian border after that, only to be told, where are your papers?
Your-- and they told-- explained the situation, that we were escapees and all this.
And anyway, the British said, no, that's not an order.
You've got to have certificates.
We have other people waiting that want to come.
You can't come in.
You have to go back, this, that, and the other.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I don't know.
You don't recall any of that.
No, I just was--
She had a-- No.
She had a daughter with her.
But I don't know anything about her--
--her private life.
Although, I knew her very well.
And I spoke to her when I-- on a number of occasions.
Recha Freier, in fact, it is claimed, and I believe it so, was the founder of Youth Aliyah, which is the international movement.

And she later on went on to rescue thousands and thousands of children, picking them up off the street and bringing children in from Tehran, from all over the place, and in fact has got-- was given the Israel Prize later on and did lots and lots of good deeds.
So--
After have crossed-- after having crossed so many borders, and difficult borders, to get into Palestine wasn't really a problem.
I mean, in Rosh Hanikra, the-- or Rosh an-Nawakir as they called it, there were a few soldiers there.
But I mean, we just went back and, you know--
No, we didn't.
That was a normal way of going through.
And we just went back and fanned out across the border and just crossed in, you know?
And then gathered on the other side, and were told-- and asked, actually, did we have relatives that we wanted to go to?
Did we want to go to the city, or what our wishes were?
In my own case, I didn't want to go to relatives, even though I had some.
I had a couple of uncles there.
I said, I want to go to kibbutz.
I specified I wanted to go to a religious kibbut.
It turned out, it was a traditional one, not a religious one.
And I finished up in Mishmar Hasharon with a few of my friends.
That was the kibbutz.
Vitus was still there.
And so were a couple of others.
And we were put together with a group of other young children.
Some of 'em had come via Sweden.
And we formed a group, a Youth Aliyah group and stayed there for two years on Mishmar Hasharon.
During that time, we had Hebrew lessons.
We had some other [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] lessons, you know, other educational things.
And of course--
No.
No.
We--
Well, we had-- yeah.
You know, we learned history and this.
And that was all after doing a day's work.
Well, on the kibbutz, different people were doing different things.
Vitus was working in the [INAUDIBLE], which is-- the [INAUDIBLE] is where the chicken--
--were.

I started working with horses, and then graduated to the garage and finished up learning to become a mechanic and work on tractors and all this type of mechanical stuff, which was sort of the thing that I enjoyed.
And we put a lot of hard work in.
And, of course, we had a social life.
But, of course, it was all tainted at that time with the thoughts, what are we doing here?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did we leave our families behind?
What's happening to them, and so forth and so forth?
And of course, many of us never found out.
Some of them did.
Did you have any contact at this point-- obviously, not while you were traveling.
No.
Not at all.
Well, I tried to make contact once I settled in.
There was no contact whilst I-- we were traveling.
No, of course not.
Once you settled in to Mishmar Hasharon.
Yes.
There was a facility of writing letters through the Red Cross.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was contacted then by my father-- who I'm not sure whether he was in Australia.
I think he was in Australia already then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who had been told by my mother where I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aiming to go.
And then I wrote to my mother.
So I'm not quite sure whether she already-- she knew that I had arrived or not.
But I got a letter from him, because I had the uncles there.
And they would tell him that I was there.
And that contact was really done sort of through-- through my mother, who was still in Aken Elbe until 19-- well, this was 1942.
And I have a letter here that was the last letter that was sent to her at her then known address.
It came back with a stamp over it to say address unknown or undeliverable, couldn't be delivered, where the postman had actually written [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in pencil on it, [GERMAN].
I'll say it in English.
[GERMAN]
[GERMAN], which means sent to the, I guess, it's concentration camp in Warsaw.
And that was the last we heard of her.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, I think it was a letter sent by my father.
I'm not sure.
I've got it here.
Anyway, I also got my letters back.
But I mean, I have a few of them, so I've just sort of picked out the one of them.
And so, that's the story.
I then lived-- we stayed in-- in- in Israel, in Palestine.
A few of my friends who managed to put their age up, managed to join the Jewish Brigade, and then go back to fight the Germans with the Jewish brigade and liberate--
Jewish Brigade was a brigade of Palestinian Jewish citizens.
I'm using Palestinian here.
Israeli, because Israel was not in existence then-- formed as part of I think the 8th Army.
And they were sent back there to fight, you know, and liberate Europe.
And they were able to help out.
And they worked with translators and liberate camps and all that sort of thing.
I wasn't able to get my age up for them to believe me to do that.
Because that's what everybody wanted to do.
Well, everybody--
--would want to do that.
But on the other hand, there were other things.
It was, you know-- there were many things that had to be done.
Because during that time, this was a time when the immigrant ships were arriving.
And in the meantime, the Haganah had been formed.
And, of course, as members of the kibbutz and young members, we became part of the Haganah.
We were trained at the time with guns and all the things that go with it.
Also, in readiness for the creation of the state, which would come eventually, there was the Palmach, you know, that when they are doing things.
And there's all sorts of stories.
But I'd need another 10 tapes to tell you.
That was the elite group of the Haganah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] captions of--
Yeah.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were the Red Berets, if you like, you know.
You're doing special jobs of blowing up bridges, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anything that needs to be done, which is the commanders.
I was looking for that word.
So we were being trained in that.
And, of course, I was working as a tractorist in those days.
I was given a job as a personnel driver of-- I was working in a camp in [INAUDIBLE].
And I was in a driver of the commandant there.
And--
No, no.
This was an English camp.
In Israel.
We're now-- we've gone on to--
No, sorry. '45.
This was during the war.
Sorry, this was '44, yeah, I think.
Yes.
Yes.
He--
That he was in camp here.
And we were busy with those things.
And, of course, our job was to divert the English army.
That was our job at the time.
If we knew that a ship was coming in like the Exodus and all these immigrant ships--
--was to divert the British soldiers.
If they were coming in-- in [INAUDIBLE], we had to divert them, make them believe that they're coming in on the other side.
And then, of course, people would go out.
They would change clothes with them.
They would give them local clothes.
They would take them into kibbutzim.
This was a whole time.
These were the immigrant ships that were coming in.
And we became very busy in all that sort of action.
From Italy, from all over Europe, you know.
And there was the [INAUDIBLE].
Later on, they actually--
Yeah, Jewish immigrants.
Yes.
But later on, what they did later on is they-- they intercepted them.
This is a whole different story.
They intercept them and send them all to Cyprus to be interned there.
And then gradually they came back there.
I can't go into all this now.
So then I stayed on the kibbutz for two years.
I became [LAUGHS] expert in-- in building roads and airfields and fish ponds and all that.
And I worked for a cooperative on behalf of the kibbutz for one year, and then joined the cooperative as an independent worker for a while, traveled all over the country.
One of the conditions of my employment was that I work for 12 months for the Arabs.
Because there were-- after the war, there was a shortage of tractors.
And the English government says, OK, we have 24 tractors.
We will give you 20 off them, providing you use half of them to work half the hours for the Arab population.
And I was one of the guys that used to travel around from place to place doing that.
And in fact, I learned quite a bit of Arabic.
But I've since forgotten.
No-no-no, this was just- Yeah.
This was '44, '45.
No, she-- I knew that she had been sent to Warsaw ghetto and--
Well, because it was on a letter there.
But I also know that-- I found out from my aunt who stayed after the war who had been sending her parcels there.
That aunt is still alive today in Cleveland, Ohio.
And my mother actually said to her, don't send us anymore at a certain date, you know.
Because she still had the children with her.
We are going.
Don't send us anymore parcels, because we're going to be shipped to-- I forget now she said Auschwitz or one of the camps or, next week.
And don't bother, you know?
So that's all I know.
I've got not date or time or place of when they actually perished.
But you found that out later through her sister about your mum and your brother and sister?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Two sisters had survived.
And then I went out and I went into business by myself for a while.
I bought myself a--
